content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 20, 2017

Jennifer Chmura
Attorney IV
California Department of Health Care Services
Office of Legal Services
P.O. Box 997413, MS 0010
Sacramento, CA 95899-7413

Re:    Your Request for Informal Assistance
       Our File No. I-17-051

Dear Ms. Chmura:

     This letter responds to your request for advice regarding conflict of interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

     Because your questions are general in nature, we are treating your request as one for
informal assistance. Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided
by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

     Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of
interest or Section 1090.

                                              FACTS

     You are an attorney IV with the California Department of Health Care Services, and your
duties relate to the Medi-Cal program and the provision of its services. You have been a state
employee since 2002. Throughout this employment, you have owned an individual retirement
account "IRA" rollover account, administered by Charles Schwab & Co., that includes Vanguard
Equity Funds and 650 B class shares of Berkshire Hathaway Inc., which you disclose on your 700
Form. Berkshire Hathaway owns General Reinsurance ("General Re") which reinsures health care
entities. These shares have a current value of $174.40 per share, and with approximately 2.467
billion shares outstanding or in the market.²


_________________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² https://ycharts.com/companies/BRK.B/key_stats

File No. I-17-051
Page No. 2

Generally, Berkshire Hathaway is a holding company for subsidiaries involved in the
manufacturing, retail, and service industries. Insurance, conducted on both a primary and
reinsurance basis, constitutes Berkshire Hathaway's most important business. GEICO Corporation
and General Reinsurance are the company's principal insurance subsidiaries. The company's other
subsidiaries are involved in a variety of businesses.

Your duties as counsel include the following:

• review draft contracts (drafted by Program staff) for legal compliance before they are
released.
• legal review and approval of contracting out justifications prepared by the programs.
• draft form language inserted into trailer bill language allowing DHCS to contract out for
services
• evaluate whether specific Medi-Cal providers (individuals and entities) have engaged in
fraud and abuse, and if they have violated the Medi-Cal and Medicaid laws, regulations, and
provider agreements.
• determine if a company that changed ownership qualified to participate under Federal law as
a "provider."

You have not been involved in:

• reviewing contract/bid submissions or in determining who is awarded the contract, as this
task is performed by Program staff.

To your knowledge your duties have not involved any transaction that impacted General Re
or Berkshire Hathaway.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official knows or reason to know he or she has a financial interest. Section 87103 defines
financial interests to include:

Section 87103 defines financial interests to include, as relevant here:

• Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth at
least $2,000 and any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer,
partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management.

• Any source of income, such as a business entity, from which the official has received
income of $500 or more within 12 months before the decision. This also includes income
from any client of the business entity of at least $500, provided to and received by the public
official within 12 months before the decision is made.

File No. I-17-051
Page No. 3

• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule.

Our focus is on your financial interest in a business entity due to your investment in 650 Berkshire Hathaway Class B shares. An "investment" in a business entity includes a financial interest in common stock, where the business entity, or any subsidiary, or otherwise related business entity does business in the jurisdiction and has a fair market value of $2,000. (Section 82034.) An "investment" does not include a fund held in a qualified retirement plan such as a 401k, 403b or 457 or similar "diversified mutual fund registered with the Securities and Exchange Commission under the Investment Company Act of 1940" under certain conditions. (Regulation 18237.) Your holding of Berkshire Hathaway stock is an investment pursuant to Section 82034. The exception for qualified retirement plans or a similar mutual fund plan does not apply to your IRA, which holds stock in two companies and is under your control. (See Regulation 18237.)

To determine if you will have a prohibited conflict of interest under the Act, we examine if it is reasonably foreseeable that a governmental decision will have a material financial effect on your shares of stock. (Regulation 18700(d).) The following analysis is general, as no particular decision is currently at issue.

Foreseeability and Materiality:

Explicitly Involved

Regulation 18701 sets forth the two standards for determining "foreseeability" depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in a particular decision. A financial interest is explicitly involved where it is a "named party" or the "subject" of a decision. (Regulation 18701(a).) The financial interest is the "subject" of a decision where the government decision "involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest." (Id.) A financial interest that is explicitly involved is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable. (Id.)

For decisions where Berkshire Hathaway is explicitly involved in the decision, Regulation 18702.1(a) sets forth the materiality standard. Generally, the materiality standard will be met if the business entity initiates, or is subject to, the action taken by the agency; and the effect is directed solely at the business entity. "Business entity" here would include Berkshire Hathaway and any of its related entities. We recommend that you seek additional advice in the event that the business entity appears to be explicitly involved in an upcoming decision.

Not Explicitly Involved

Regulation 18701(b) sets out the appropriate standard to determine foreseeability where the financial interest is not explicitly involved in the decision. Generally, a financial effect is reasonably foreseeable where it "can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical . . . absent extraordinary circumstances beyond the public official's control." (Regulation 18701(b).) A financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. (Id.)

File No. 1-17-051
Page No. 4

Regulation 1870l(b) provides some general guidelines to consider to determine
foreseeability:

(1) The extent to which the occurrence of the financial effect is contingent upon
intervening events, not including future governmental decisions by the official's
agency, or any other agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary control of
the official's agency.

(2) Whether the public official should anticipate a financial effect on his or her
financial interest as a potential outcome under normal circumstances when using
appropriate due diligence and care.

(3) Whether the public official has a financial interest that is of the type that
would typically be affected by the terms of the governmental decision or whether
the governmental decision is of the type that would be expected to have a
financial effect on businesses and individuals similarly situated to those
businesses and individuals in which the public official has a financial interest.

(4) Whether a reasonable inference can be made that the financial effects of the
governmental decision on the public official's financial interest might
compromise a public official's ability to act in a manner consistent with his or her
duty to act in the best interests of the public.

(5) Whether the governmental decision will provide or deny an opportunity, or
create an advantage or disadvantage for one of the official's financial interests,
including whether the financial interest may be entitled to compete or be eligible
for a benefit resulting from the decision.

(6) Whether the public official has the type of financial interest that would cause a
similarly situated person to weigh the advantages and disadvantages of the
governmental decision on his or her financial interest in formulating a position.

The standard to determine materiality, where a business entity is not explicitly involved is
set forth in Regulation 18702.l(b).) Materiality is found if "a prudent person with sufficient
information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would
contribute to a change in the price of the business entity's publicly traded stock." (Regulation
18702.l(b).)

Examples of decisions that may be applicable include, as relevant, those that:

(1) Authorize, prohibit, regulate or otherwise establish conditions for an activity in which
the business entity is engaged;

(2) Increase or decrease the amount of competition in the field in which the business entity
is engaged;

File No. I-17-051
Page No. 5

(3) Increase or decrease the need for the products or services that the business entity
supplies;

[ . . . ]

(6) Increase or decrease the tax burden, debt, or financial or legal liability of the business
entity.

(Regulation 18702.1(b).)

Please note that the determination of a conflict of interest is dependent upon the facts
involved in a particular decision. We recommend you contact our office in the event that you are
concerned about a particular decision, given the information provided above.

Also, please be aware that Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in
their official capacities, from participating in the making of contracts in which they are financially
interested. Section 1090 is concerned with financial interests, other than remote or minimal
interests, that prevent public officials from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in
furthering the best interests of their agencies. Please contact us for additional advice in the event
that a contract is at issue, in which you have a financial interest. As a general matter, small
ownership interests in stock may be deemed a "non-interest" if the interest is:

(1) The ownership of less than 3 percent of the shares of a corporation for profit,
provided that the total annual income to him or her from dividends, including the
value of stock dividends, from the corporation does not exceed 5 percent of his or
her total annual income, and any other payments made to him or her by the
corporation do not exceed 5 percent of his or her total annual income.

(Section 1091.5(a)(1).)

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: L. Karen Harrison
Counsel, Legal Division

LKH:jgl

sections.analysis:
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or 
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the 
official knows or has reason to know he or she has a financial interest. Section 8 7103 defines 
financial interests to include: 
Section 87103 defines financial interests to include, as relevant here: 
• Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth at 
least $2,000 and any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, 
partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. 
• Any source of income, such as a business entity, from which the official has received 
income of $500 or more within 12 months before the decision. This also includes income 
from any client of the business entity of at least $500, provided to and received by the public 
official within 12 months before the decision is made. 


FileNo. 1-17-051  
• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate 
family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule. 
Our focus is on your financial interest in a business entity due to your investment in 650 
Berkshire Hathaway Class B shares. An "investment" in a business entity includes a financial 
interest in common stock, where the business entity, or any subsidiary, or otherwise related business 
entity does business in the jurisdiction and has a fair market value of $2,000. (Section 82034.) An 
"investment" does not include a fund held in a qualified retirement plan such as a 401 k, 403b or 457 
or similar "diversified mutual fund registered with the Securities and Exchange Commission under 
the Investment Company Act of 1940" under certain conditions. (Regulation 18237.) Your holding 
of Berkshire Hathaway stock is an investment pursuant to Section 82034. The exception for 
qualified retirement plans or a similar mutual fund plan does not apply to your IRA, which holds 
stock in two companies and is under your control. (See Regulation 1823 7.) 
To determine if you will have a prohibited conflict of interest under the Act, we examine if 
it is reasonably foreseeable that a governmental decision will have a material financial effect on 
your chares of stock. (Regulation 18700(d).) The following analysis is general, as no particular 
decision is currently at issue. 
Foreseeability and Materiality: 
Explicitly Involved 
Regulation 18701 sets forth the two standards for determining "foreseeability" depending on 
whether an interest is explicitly involved in a particular decision. A financial interest is explicitly 
involved where it is a "named party" or the "subject" of a decision. (Regulation 18701 (a).) The 
financial interest is the "subject" of a decision where the government decision "involves the 
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or 
contract with, the financial interest." (Id.) A financial interest that is explicitly involved is presumed 
to be reasonably foreseeable. (Id.) 
For decisions where Berkshire Hathaway is explicitly involved in the decision, Regulation 
18702.1 (a) sets forth the materiality standard. Generally, the materiality standard will be met if the 
business entity initiates, or is subject to, the action taken by the agency; and the effect is directed 
solely at the business entity. "Business entity" here would include Berkshire Hathaway and any of 
its related entities. We recommend that you seek additional advice in the event that the business 
entity appears to be explicitly involved in an upcoming decision. 
Not Explicitly Involved 
Regulation 18701 (b) sets out the appropriate standard to determine foreseeability where the 
financial interest is not explicitly involved in the decision. Generally, a financial effect is 
reasonably foreseeable where it "can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than 
hypothetical or theoretical . . .  absent extraordinary circumstances beyond the public official's 
control." (Regulation 1870l(b).) A financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably 
foreseeable. (Id.)  
Regulation 1870l(b) provides some general guidelines to consider to detennine 
foreseeability: 
(1) The extent to which the occurrence of the financial effect is contingent upon 
intervening events, not including future governmental decisions by the official's 
agency, or any other agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary control of 
the official's agency. 
(2) Whether the public official should anticipate a financial effect on his or her 
financial interest as a potential outcome under nonnal circumstances when using 
appropriate due diligence and care. 
(3) Whether the public official has a financial interest that is of the type that 
would typically be affected by the tenns of the governmental decision or whether 
the governmental decision is of the type that would be expected to have a 
financial effect on businesses and individuals similarly situated to those 
businesses and individuals in which the public official has a financial interest. 
(4) Whether a reasonable inference can be made that the financial effects of the 
governmental decision on the public official's financial interest might 
compromise a public official's ability to act in a manner consistent with his or her 
duty to act in the best interests of the public. 
(5) Whether the governmental decision will provide or deny an opportunity, or 
create an advantage or disadvantage for one of the official's financial interests, 
including whether the financial interest may be entitled to compete or be eligible 
for a benefit resulting from the decision. 
( 6) Whether the public official has the type of financial interest that would cause a 
similarly situated person to weigh the advantages and disadvantages of the 
governmental decision on his or her financial interest in formulating a position. 
The standard to determine materiality, where a business entity is not explicitly involved is 
set forth in Regulation 18702.1 (b).) Materiality is found if "a prudent person with sufficient 
infonnation would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would 
contribute to a change in the price of the business entity's publicly traded stock." (Regulation 
18702.l(b).) 
Examples of decisions that may be applicable include, as relevant, those that: 
(1) Authorize, prohibit, regulate or otherwise establish conditions for an activity in which 
the business entity is engaged; 
(2) Increase or decrease the amount of competition in the field in which the business entity 
is engaged;  
{3) Increase or decrease the need for the products or services that the business entity 
supplies; 
[ . . .  ] 
(6) Increase or decrease the tax burden, debt, or financial or legal liability of the business 
entity. 
(Regulation I 8702.1 (h).) 
Please note that the detennination of a conflict of interest is dependent upon the facts 
involved in a particular decision. We recommend you contact our office in the event that you are 
concerned about a particular decision, given the information provided above. 
Also, please be aware that Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in 
their official capacities, from participating in the making of contracts in which they are financially 
interested. Section 1090 is concerned with financial interests, other than remote or minimal 
interests, that prevent public officials from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in 
furthering the best interests of their agencies. Please contact us for additional advice in the event 
that a contract is at issue, in which you have a financial interest. As a general matter, small 
ownership interests in stock may be deemed a "non-interest" if the interest is: 
(1) The ownership ofless than 3 percent of the shares of a corporation for profit, 
provided that the total annual income to him or her from dividends, including the 
value of stock dividends, from the corporation does not exceed 5 percent of his or 
her total annual income, and any other payments made to him or her by the 
corporation do not exceed 5 percent of his or her total annual income. 
{Section 1091.S{a)(l).)
sections.question_synthetic: Does an attorney at the California Department of Health Care Services have a prohibited conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act by owning 650 shares of Berkshire Hathaway stock in an IRA rollover account, given that Berkshire Hathaway owns General Reinsurance which reinsures health care entities?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that whether a conflict of interest exists depends on whether a governmental decision has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the Berkshire Hathaway shares. For explicitly involved decisions, the requestor should seek additional advice; for other decisions, foreseeability requires more than hypothetical effects and materiality requires a prudent person would find a realistic possibility of affecting stock price. The requestor should contact the FPPC regarding specific decisions.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Informal advice letter (not formal opinion); no formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections but analysis provides advice on foreseeability and materiality standards; document uses FACTS and ANALYSIS structure; notes that Section 1090 conflict analysis also applies; advice is general in nature and does not provide immunity

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does an attorney at the California Department of Health Care Services have a prohibited conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act by owning 650 shares of Berkshire Hathaway stock in an IRA rollover account, given that Berkshire Hathaway owns General Reinsurance which reinsures health care entities?

CONCLUSION: The FPPC concludes that whether a conflict of interest exists depends on whether a governmental decision has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the Berkshire Hathaway shares. For explicitly involved decisions, the requestor should seek additional advice; for other decisions, foreseeability requires more than hypothetical effects and materiality requires a prudent person would find a realistic possibility of affecting stock price. The requestor should contact the FPPC regarding specific decisions.

embedding.summary: The FPPC provides informal advice to a DHCS attorney regarding potential conflict of interest issues arising from her ownership of Berkshire Hathaway stock in a retirement account, analyzing foreseeability and materiality standards under the Political Reform Act.
